title: EV vs petrol: the five-year cost of ownership
description: Full five-year cost comparison of an EV versus a petrol car in the UK: fuel, tax, servicing, depreciation and the 2028 pay-per-mile charge, with real figures.
blurb: The honest five-year sums for EV versus petrol: fuel, tax, servicing, depreciation and what the 2028 mileage charge changes.
lede: Ask whether an electric car is cheaper than petrol and you will get two confident, opposite answers. EV owners point at 2p-a-mile overnight charging; sceptics point at higher purchase prices, steep early depreciation and 79p-a-kWh rapid chargers. Both are describing real cars, just different ones. The only fair way to settle it is to add up everything an owner pays over a realistic period: energy, road tax, insurance, servicing, the charge point, and the value lost when the car is sold. This guide does exactly that over five years and 40,000 miles, using current UK prices, and shows which side of the line your own situation falls on.
short_answer: Over five years and 40,000 miles, an EV charged at home on an off-peak tariff costs about £3,300 to £3,900 in electricity, tax, servicing and a charge point, against £8,900 to £9,400 for an equivalent petrol car: a running-cost saving of roughly £5,000 to £6,000. Charging at the standard price-cap rate cuts that saving to around £3,000 to £3,500, and relying on public rapid chargers can erase it entirely. Purchase price and depreciation then decide the overall winner, which is why a used EV charged at home is currently the strongest value in UK motoring.

sections.h2: What a five-year comparison has to include
sections.html:
Most EV-versus-petrol arguments only compare fuel, and fuel is the cost where the EV wins by the widest margin. A proper total cost of ownership adds up six things:

  • Energy. Petrol at the pump versus electricity at whatever rate you actually charge at. We cover the per-mile maths in detail in our guide to EV running costs versus petrol.
  • Depreciation. The gap between what you pay and what you sell for. For new cars this is usually the single biggest cost, bigger than fuel.
  • Road tax (VED). Since April 2025 EVs pay VED too, so the gap here is now small.
  • Insurance. Often somewhat higher for EVs, though the gap has narrowed.
  • Servicing, MOT and tyres. EVs need less routine work but wear tyres slightly faster.
  • One-off costs. A home charge point for the EV, typically £800 to £1,200 installed.

One thing deliberately excluded: finance. Interest costs scale with the price of whatever you borrow for, and they vary so much between deals that a generic figure would mislead. If you finance either car, add the total interest from your own quote to that side of the ledger.

Here is the core comparison: a typical petrol family hatchback doing 45 mpg against an equivalent EV doing 3.5 miles per kWh, both covering 8,000 miles a year for five years. The EV appears twice, because the tariff you charge on changes its costs more than the car itself does.

Cost over 5 years / 40,000 milesPetrol, 45 mpgEV, off-peak home chargingEV, price-cap rate
Fuel or electricity£6,425£800£2,985
Road tax (VED)£1,200 to £1,350£810£810
Servicing, MOT and consumables£1,300 to £1,600£850 to £1,100£850 to £1,100
Home charge point£0£800 to £1,200£800 to £1,200
Five-year total£8,900 to £9,400£3,300 to £3,900£5,400 to £6,100

Petrol at 159p per litre (mid 2026); electricity at 7p per kWh on an off-peak EV tariff or 26.11p per kWh (Ofgem cap, July to September 2026); EV efficiency 3.5 miles per kWh; VED at 2026-27 rates for new cars under the expensive car supplement thresholds.

The headline: a home-charged EV on an off-peak tariff saves £5,000 to £6,000 in running costs over five years, and even charging at the full price-cap rate it saves £3,000 to £3,500. The £800 electricity figure is not a stunt number either; several EV tariffs offer overnight rates of 7p to 9p, and the cheapest smart tariffs go as low as 5.5p, which would bring five years of charging down to about £630. Our guide to home EV charging costs shows how to work out your own figure from your tariff and your car's efficiency.

Running costs favour the EV. Depreciation is where petrol has fought back, and it deserves honest treatment because for new cars it dwarfs everything in the table above. A new car of either type typically loses half to two thirds of its value over five years, which on a £35,000 purchase means £18,000 to £23,000 gone, three to six times the entire running-cost saving.

EVs had a brutal spell here. Used electric values fell sharply between 2023 and 2025 as early prices corrected and supply grew, so plenty of first owners lost more on depreciation than they saved on fuel. The picture in 2026 is steadier: used EV prices have largely found their level, and that crash is now the used buyer's gain. A three or four year old EV has already taken its biggest value hit, often sells for a similar price to its petrol equivalent, and still delivers the full 2p-a-mile charging saving.

So apply the method rather than a slogan. For the specific cars you are comparing, look up realistic five-year-old values in the used listings, subtract from the price you would actually pay, and put that depreciation figure alongside the running costs. For a new EV, also check the Electric Car Grant: at the time of writing it gives up to £3,750 off eligible new EVs priced at £37,000 or under, applied automatically at the dealer. Grant amounts and the eligible-car list change, so check the current rules on GOV.UK before you rely on it. A grant taken at purchase is depreciation someone else has paid for.

sections.h2: Your charging mix sets the fuel saving
sections.html:
The table's two EV columns hint at the bigger truth: there is no single EV fuel cost, only a cost per mile for each place you plug in. At 3.5 miles per kWh:

  • Off-peak home charging at 7p: 2p per mile
  • Home charging at the 26.11p cap rate: 7.5p per mile
  • Public standard chargers at about 54p: 15.4p per mile
  • Public rapid chargers at about 79p: 22.6p per mile
  • Petrol at 159p per litre and 45 mpg: about 16p per mile

Read that list carefully and the whole argument resolves. Charge at home overnight and you drive for an eighth of the cost of petrol. Rely on rapid chargers and you pay more per mile than a petrol car, a point we unpack in public EV charging costs explained. Most owners sit between the extremes: perhaps 85 to 90% home charging with occasional rapid top-ups on long trips, which still lands around 4p to 5p per mile.

Two practical consequences. First, if you have a driveway, the off-peak tariff is not optional extra credit, it is most of the financial case; EV electricity tariffs explained covers how these tariffs work and what to look for. Second, if you cannot charge at home or at work, run the five-year sums with public rates before buying, because the famous EV saving may simply not exist for you. Budget a winter allowance too: cold weather cuts EV efficiency by roughly 15 to 25%, so real-world annual costs sit a little above the summer figure.

sections.h2: Tax, insurance and the 2028 pay-per-mile plan
sections.html:
The EV tax holiday is over, but the gap has not reversed. Since April 2025 EVs pay VED: £10 in the first year for a new car, then the standard rate, which is £200 a year in 2026-27, the same standard rate petrol cars pay. Petrol cars still pay more in year one, since first-year VED is scaled to CO2 and typically runs £400 to £560 for a mainstream hatchback and far more for thirstier engines. The expensive car supplement adds £440 a year in years two to six for costlier cars, and here EVs get a concession: the threshold is a £50,000 list price for EVs against £40,000 for petrol.

The genuinely new factor is the proposed electric vehicle excise duty, a pay-per-mile charge announced in the November 2025 Budget and planned for April 2028 at 3p per mile for EVs (1.5p for plug-in hybrids). At 8,000 miles a year that would add £240 a year, around £500 to £700 across the final years of a five-year window starting now. It trims the EV's advantage; it comes nowhere near closing a £1,000-a-year fuel gap. The scheme was still being finalised after a consultation that closed in March 2026, so check the current position on GOV.UK before building it into your sums.

Insurance is the EV's other soft spot. Premiums have historically run 10 to 25% higher for EVs, driven by repair costs, though the gap has been narrowing as parts supply and repairer experience improve. It varies hugely by model and postcode, so get real quotes for both cars before deciding; a bad insurance match can eat £1,000 or more of the five-year saving. Servicing leans the other way: no oil changes, no clutch, no exhaust, and regenerative braking that makes pads and discs last far longer, partly offset by slightly faster tyre wear on heavier EVs. And the battery scare stories deserve retiring: modern packs routinely outlast the car, as we show in how long EV batteries last.

Put it all together and the answer splits cleanly by situation rather than by tribe.

  • Driveway plus off-peak tariff: the EV wins, and not narrowly. £5,000 to £6,000 lower running costs over five years buys a lot of headroom on price, and a used EV bought after the early depreciation hit wins on every line.
  • Home charging at standard rates: still a clear EV win on running costs, around £3,000 to £3,500 over five years, though switching to an EV tariff would roughly double it for an evening of admin.
  • No home or workplace charging: genuinely marginal. Public rapid charging costs more per mile than petrol, so the case rests on cheaper servicing, kerbside or workplace schemes appearing, or non-financial reasons.
  • Buying new at a premium: do the depreciation sum before celebrating the fuel saving. A £6,000 higher purchase price on a fast-depreciating model can swallow five years of cheap charging.

Three checks before you commit: price the exact used values of the cars you are comparing rather than trusting averages, get insurance quotes for both, and confirm you can actually get an off-peak tariff at your property, because that single line item carries most of the EV's case. Do those and the five-year answer stops being an argument and becomes arithmetic.

faq.q: Is an electric car really cheaper than petrol over five years?
faq.a: On running costs, yes, by a wide margin if you charge at home. Over 40,000 miles an off-peak-charged EV costs about £3,300 to £3,900 in electricity, tax, servicing and a charge point against £8,900 to £9,400 for an equivalent petrol car. The overall winner then depends on purchase price and depreciation, which is why used EVs are currently the strongest buy.

faq.q: Will the 2028 pay-per-mile charge wipe out EV savings?
faq.a: No. The proposed electric vehicle excise duty of 3p per mile from April 2028 would cost about £240 a year at 8,000 miles, against a typical home-charging fuel saving of £1,000 or more a year. It trims the advantage rather than closing it. The scheme details were still being finalised in 2026, so check GOV.UK for the current position.

faq.q: Do electric cars cost more to insure?
faq.a: Often, yes. EV premiums have historically run 10 to 25% higher, mainly because of repair costs, though the gap is narrowing. It varies a lot by model and postcode, so always get quotes for both cars you are comparing before deciding.

faq.q: Is it cheaper to buy a used EV than a used petrol car?
faq.a: Frequently. Used EV prices fell sharply between 2023 and 2025, so a three or four year old EV often costs about the same as its petrol equivalent while still delivering charging costs of around 2p per mile on an off-peak tariff. The biggest depreciation has already been paid by the first owner.

faq.q: What if I cannot charge at home?
faq.a: Run the sums with public charging prices before buying. Public rapid chargers at around 79p per kWh cost about 22.6p per mile, more than petrol at roughly 16p per mile, and standard public chargers at about 54p are only slightly cheaper than petrol. Without home or workplace charging, an EV's financial case is marginal.
